These losses remain within the fund and can be used to offset future capital gains or reduce the tax liability of the fund in subsequent years. Mutual funds are required to distribute capital gains to shareholders, but they do not have to distribute losses. Instead, these losses can be carried forward to offset future gains, potentially reducing the amount of capital gains distributions to shareholders in the future. This mechanism helps in managing the tax implications for the fund and its investors.

Why naked call writing is risky compare to Covered call? Why naked call writing is risky compare to Covered call? Naked call writing is considered riskier compared to covered call writing due to the potential for unlimited losses. In naked call writing, the investor sells a call option without owning the underlying asset. If the price of the underlying asset rises significantly above the strike price, the buyer of the call option can exercise the option, forcing the seller to buy the asset at the current market price and sell it at the lower strike price. This can result in substantial losses as the market price can theoretically rise indefinitely.
